results.possibleIdentity: Stan VanDerBeek (1927–1984) was a prolific American experimental filmmaker, animator, and multimedia artist who was a key figure in the avant-garde and expanded cinema movements of the 1960s and 1970s. He is widely recognized for his pioneering role in the intersection of art and technology, specifically through his early use of computer graphics, video, and cross-media communications. VanDerBeek was closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), collaborating with engineers such as Kenneth C. Knowlton at Bell Telephone Laboratories to create some of the first computer-animated films, including the influential 'Poem Field' series (1964–1970). In the mid-1960s, he constructed the 'Movie Drome' in Stony Point, New York—a geodesic dome designed for immersive multimedia projections that he envisioned as a global 'experience machine' for visual communication. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he held several prestigious residencies, including a fellowship at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) and a residency at the public television station WGBH, where he produced 'Violence Sonata' (1970) and used fax machines to create large-scale collaborative 'Telephone Murals' or 'Panels for the Walls of the World.' His work often explored utopian themes, aiming to use technology to facilitate a new universal language and world understanding.

During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he held several prestigious residencies, including a fellowship at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) and a residency at the public television station WGBH, where he produced 'Violence Sonata' (1970) and used fax machines to create large-scale collaborative 'Telephone Murals' or 'Panels for the Walls of the World.'

results.possibleIdentity: Joan W. Harris is a prominent American arts administrator and philanthropist based in Chicago, recognized with the National Medal of Arts in 2013. Active in the arts community since the late 1960s, she served as the Commissioner of the Department of Cultural Affairs for the City of Chicago and as the president of the Irving Harris Foundation. While she is primarily known as an arts patron and leader, she began her journey in the arts as a pianist and has been a driving force behind major cultural projects that bridge art, architecture, and technology, such as the development of Millennium Park and the Harris Theater for Music and Dance. Her work often involves collaborative initiatives between artists and civic institutions, aligning with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Joan Harris is an artist and scientific illustrator who has been active since at least 1967. Born in 1946, she earned her B.A. in 1968 and has worked as a freelance illustrator specializing in scientific illustration, a field that emphasizes the precise intersection of art, accuracy, and technical detail. Her career began during the late 1960s, a period coinciding with the rise of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. She is a member of the Guild of Scientific Illustrators and is known for her detailed graphite drawings, particularly a series focusing on the rehabilitation of wild birds in Alaska. This technical and collaborative approach to art and science is characteristic of the projects promoted by E.A.T. during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Joan Harris was a photographer or documentarian active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is credited with capturing significant photographic records of major contemporary artists, including portraits of Agnes Martin taken between 1967 and 1968. Given that Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was based in New York and relied heavily on photographers to document its groundbreaking collaborations between artists and engineers, she may have been associated with the organization's documentary efforts during its most active period.

results.possibleIdentity: Bettina Brendel (1922–2009) was a German-born American abstract painter and printmaker known for her lifelong artistic investigation into the intersection of art and science, particularly physics. Born in Lüneburg, Germany, she was the daughter of the Expressionist poet Dr. Robert Brendel. After studying at the State Academy of Art in Hamburg (1946–1948) and exhibiting with early postwar abstractionists in Munich, she immigrated to the United States in 1951 and became a citizen in 1957. Brendel settled in Los Angeles in 1955, where she studied at the University of Southern California and taught at UCLA Extension. In the late 1960s, she moved to New York to study the history and theory of physics at the New School for Social Research (1968–1969), a pursuit that deeply informed her work on subatomic particles, wave-particle duality, and quantum mechanics. During this period, she was active in the emerging 'Art and Technology' movement, contributing to the journal Leonardo and engaging with leading scientists, including a 1972 meeting with Nobel physicist Werner Heisenberg. Her work, which later incorporated computer-generated imagery and binary code themes, has been featured in major exhibitions such as 'Particles and Waves: Bettina Brendel' (1998) at the Long Beach Museum of Art and the Getty-led PST ART exhibition 'Particles and Waves: Southern California Abstraction and Science, 1945–1990' (2024–2025).

results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer was a Romanian-born sculptress active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily known for her association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Born around 1930, she was involved in E.A.T.'s Technical Services Program, which launched in 1967 to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. In 1968, she participated in the exhibition "Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes" at the Brooklyn Museum, collaborating with artist Ben-Ami Kadish. Her work often utilized modern metal and technical materials; for instance, she unveiled a modern metal sculpture at the Hillel Center in New Brunswick, New Jersey, in May 1972. While she is recognized in archives focusing on women's participation in early tech-art movements, relatively little detailed biographical information survives beyond her exhibition credits and regional activity in the New York and New Jersey areas.

results.possibleIdentity: Megan Bierman is a multi-disciplinary improviser, musician, dancer, and visual artist who has been active since the late 1960s. Based in the San Francisco Bay Area, she is known for her work in music (specifically the tenor saxophone), dance, storytelling, drawing, and painting. She attended Bennington College from 1968 to 1972, where she was a member of the Judith Dunn – Bill Dixon Dance Company and participated in various experimental performances, including the Bill Dixon Ensemble's live recordings. In the 1970s, she collaborated with Laurel Sprigg, John Gruntfest, and Joe Sabella on a project titled 'Megacycles.' She has a long-standing career as an artist-in-residence at the Buen Dia Family School in San Francisco, spanning over 27 years, and was a member of the musical storytelling duo 'The Echo Sisters' during the 1980s. Bierman is the daughter of philosopher Arthur Bierman and former San Francisco Supervisor Sue Bierman, and she continues to collaborate on artistic projects with her partner, musician John Gruntfest.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Straley (1927–2008) was a New Jersey-based representational painter and gallery owner active in the 1960s, 1970s, and 1980s. He is known for his masterfully simplified portraits and figures, often rendered in oil pastels, which have been compared to the Art Brut style of Jean Dubuffet. Archival records from the Brooklyn Museum include correspondence and memos from 1970–1972 related to Straley within the context of 'Art and Technology' projects, potentially linking him to the contemporary movement of artistic and technological collaboration. He owned and operated the Straley Art Gallery in Livingston, New Jersey, during the 1970s and 1980s, where he exhibited his own work and that of other local artists like George Mueller.

Walter William Straley (1913–1999) was a high-ranking executive at AT&T and the Bell System who played a critical role as a patron and facilitator for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s. As Vice President of Public Relations at AT&T, Straley was a key supporter of E.A.T. founder Billy Klüver, authorizing corporate funding and technical resources for major collaborative projects like the 1970 Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 and the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' event. While primarily a corporate leader, Straley was an amateur photographer and a significant figure in the integration of technology and the arts; he also served as the president of the Seattle Symphony Board and was involved in the planning of the 1962 Seattle World's Fair.
